ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for jajangmyeon. FOODON_03311737 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Noodles, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for jajangmyeon or Korean black bean noodle dishes in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for jajangmyeon or Korean black bean noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of jajangmyeon with pork and egg weighs approximately 400 g, comprising noodles, black bean pork sauce, and toppings including a soft-boiled egg, cucumber, spring onions, and fried shallots.

nutrient_source.title: Jajangmyeon (Korean black bean noodles with pork) — component-based estimation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked wheat noodles, pork belly, black bean paste, egg, and vegetable toppings
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for jajangmyeon as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 55% cooked wheat noodles (USDA FDC #20121), 20% minced pork belly cooked (USDA FDC #168375), 10% black bean paste/chunjang (USDA FDC #173757 as closest proxy for fermented soybean paste), 8% soft-boiled egg (USDA FDC #748967), and 7% mixed vegetable toppings (leek, cucumber, spring onion, shallot, sesame). Sodium is elevated due to soy tare and chunjang. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for this restaurant-style dish.

gi_source.title: Noodles, instant, boiled — GI 47 (glucose reference); analogous estimate applied to wheat-based Asian noodle dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for jajangmyeon. GI of 47 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled Asian wheat noodles (GI range 35–52, representative value 47, glucose=100 reference). The black bean paste and pork components do not contribute glycemic carbohydrates and are expected to lower the overall GI slightly. GL per serving calculated as: (47 × 82 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 38.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from chunjang black bean paste and soy tare
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g bowl provides approximately 2080 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce portions or limit frequency of consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles partially offset by protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 47 (low) and GL of approximately 38.6 per bowl indicate a moderate glycaemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork belly and elevated sodium from fermented sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 9.2 g saturated fat and 2080 mg sodium per serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency and consider leaner protein substitutions to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jajangmyeon with Pork and Egg is a Korean-Chinese noodle dish consisting of thick wheat noodles coated in a rich, savoury black bean sauce (chunjang) stir-fried with minced pork belly. This restaurant version features a Korean-style preparation with soy tare for depth, smoked leek sofrito for aromatic complexity, fried shallots for crunch, toasted sesame, fresh spring onions, julienned cucumber for freshness, and a hanjuku (Japanese-style soft-boiled) egg. The dish has a deeply umami, slightly sweet, and savoury flavour profile with a glossy, thick sauce that clings to the noodles. The texture contrasts chewy noodles with tender pork, silky egg yolk, and crisp cucumber. Jajangmyeon originated from Chinese zhajiangmian and was adapted into a distinct Korean-Chinese cuisine staple, now considered a cultural comfort food in Korea often eaten on Black Day (April 14). Nutritionally, a 400 g serving provides approximately 672 kcal, 28.8 g protein, 27.2 g fat, and 82 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high at approximately 2080 mg per serving due to chunjang and soy tare, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index is estimated at 47 (low), though the glycaemic load per serving is moderate to high at approximately 38.6. The dish contains gluten, egg, soy, and sesame allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or halal diets without modification.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for jajangmyeon as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the constituent ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled Asian wheat noodles. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are individually well-supported and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. All other -1 fields are populated with estimated values from component analysis. Sodium is notably high due to chunjang (fermented black bean paste) and soy tare; the per-100g value of 520 mg reflects a restaurant-style preparation. The dish is listed under 'Chinese' cuisine in the field survey source but is more accurately classified as Korean-Chinese (a distinct fusion cuisine). The food_name has been canonicalised to 'Jajangmyeon with Pork and Egg' for database consistency; the original field survey menu name 'Jajang Pork Egg Noodles (NEW)' is preserved in aliases. Korean mandatory multilingual naming is satisfied. Chinese script names are provided as this dish has Chinese-origin roots and Chinese-script aliases are relevant for retrieval. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local_names are null as this is a Korean-Chinese dish, not a Singaporean dish; the multilingual matrix for Korean dishes requires only Korean native-script, which is satisfied.

Calculated GL per serving: (47 × 82 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 38.6.
